text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 6 October 2025 

Dr Priya Nair  
Centre for Epilepsy  
Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Priya Nair,                                                                                                         

Alex Turner, DOB: 22-11-1991, Hospital No: Q372615 NHS No. 9842175306  
Flat 3, 28 Cartwright Gardens, London, WC1H 9EL  

Epilepsy 

Medication: Currently on Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily (restarted July 2024 after a lapse), and Topiramate 50 mg nightly added March 2025 for headache and sleep consolidation.

Previously tried: Sodium Valproate (2019–2020; stopped due to weight gain and hair thinning), Carbamazepine MR (2021; stopped due to dizziness), Lamotrigine (2022; discontinued after rash), and Clobazam PRN for clusters (used intermittently; last use April 2025).

Lamotrigine Blood Level: Not applicable (no current Lamotrigine therapy).

Seizures: The patient reports fluctuation over the past 12 months with clear photosensitivity to flicker and bright light, particularly from supermarket lighting, LED screens, and sunlight through trees while travelling. They routinely use polarised sunglasses and screen filters. Sleep deprivation and missed meals continue to act as triggers. Seizures in 2024-2025: September: 7 days October: 3 days with seizures, November: 4 days, most of them at sleep time December: 7 days with seizures, January: 7 days with seizures February: 1 days with seizures March: 9 days with more severe seizures April: 10 days with seizures, May: 3 days with more severe seizures June: 6 days with more severe seizures July: 10 days, August: 10 days with seizures. Some events have been preceded by eye discomfort and brief visual shimmering when exposed to bright or flickering light; family note brief vocalisation and right arm stiffening at times, followed by confusion and fatigue lasting several hours. No clusters requiring emergency care since April 2025. No injuries. 

I reviewed Alex Turner today in face-to-face clinic. They report improved adherence since setting phone reminders and linking doses to routine meal times. They have not had alcohol for three months and are keeping a seizure diary. We discussed strategies to mitigate photosensitivity including regular breaks from screens, reduced screen brightness with blue-light filters, avoidance of strobe environments, and consistent sleep timing. They are awaiting occupational health advice for workstation lighting adjustments and have requested an employer letter; I am happy to provide this.

Investigations: MRI brain (2020) reported as normal. Routine EEG in 2019 normal; repeat EEG with intermittent photic stimulation (IPS) requested today given reported photosensitivity. Bloods (July 2025): U&E, LFT, FBC within reference ranges; Vitamin D borderline low (54 nmol/L) — supplementing over the counter. 

Plan: Continue current anti-seizure medication regimen as above without changes today. Emphasised adherence and seizure diary completion with specific notation of light exposure, sleep, and meals. Arrange EEG with IPS and consider ambulatory EEG if diary suggests nocturnal clustering. Provided DVLA guidance consistent with recent events. Patient has my clinic contact via epilepsy nurse specialist for interim concerns.

Yours sincerely

Seizures in 2024-2025: September: 7 days October: 3 days with seizures, November: 4 days, most of them at sleep time December: 7 days with seizures, January: 7 days with seizures February: 1 days with seizures March: 9 days with more severe seizures April: 10 days with seizures, May: 3 days with more severe seizures June: 6 days with more severe seizures July: 10 days, August: 10 days with seizures

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter lists seizure-day counts for each month over a 12‑month window (September to August). Extracted counts: Sep 7, Oct 3, Nov 4, Dec 7, Jan 7, Feb 1, Mar 9, Apr 10, May 3, Jun 6, Jul 10, Aug 10. Sum = 7+3+4+7+7+1+9+10+3+6+10+10 = 77 seizure days over 12 months. Therefore, the seizure frequency is 77 per 12 months.
